parts.content:
A navigation system uses a graph (consisting of nodes and weighted edges) to represent a city's road network for route planning.

Explain **three** ways this abstract representation improves the performance or functionality of the route-finding algorithm compared to using a raw, high-resolution satellite image of the city.
parts.markscheme:
_Award **[3 max]**._

- **Computational Efficiency:** By removing irrelevant visual details (e.g., building colors, vegetation), the algorithm requires significantly less processing power to calculate a path; <marks>1</marks>
- **Algorithmic Compatibility:** Converting physical roads into a mathematical model (nodes/edges) allows for the application of established pathfinding algorithms, such as Dijkstra’s, which cannot operate directly on pixel data; <marks>1</marks>
- **Memory/Storage Optimization:** The abstract model stores only essential coordinates and connectivity data, drastically reducing the memory footprint compared to large image files; <marks>1</marks>
- **Focus on Topology:** The abstraction prioritizes the relationships and distances between points (connectivity) rather than literal geographic appearance, which simplifies the logic needed to handle complex intersections or one-way streets; <marks>1</marks>
- **Scalability:** Simplified data structures allow the system to process and search through much larger geographic regions (e.g., entire continents) in real-time; <marks>1</marks>

<note>
Abstraction involves the filtering of irrelevant data to focus on the core logical structure of a problem. In this context, the "graph" is the abstract model of the "real-world city."
</note>

questions.specification: A software company is developing a new application to manage customer relationships. The application will store customer data, track interactions, and provide insights for sales teams.

parts.content: Describe the importance of data integrity in the context of the customer relationship management (CRM) application.
parts.markscheme:
- **States** that data integrity ensures customer data is accurate, consistent, and reliable, which is crucial for making informed business decisions. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explains** that it maintains trust with customers by ensuring their information is handled correctly and securely. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Outlines** how it prevents data anomalies and inconsistencies that could lead to incorrect insights and negatively impact sales strategies. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Defining Accuracy and Consistency
steps.content: Data integrity is defined as the **accuracy**, **consistency**, and **reliability** of data throughout its entire lifecycle. In the context of a CRM, this means ensuring that every piece of information stored—from a phone number to a purchase history—is correct and remains unchanged unless intentionally updated. Without this foundation, the application cannot fulfill its purpose to effectively **manage customer relationships**, as the sales team would be working with flawed information.

steps.title: Impact on Business Insights
steps.content: The CRM is designed to **provide insights for sales teams**. High data integrity prevents **data anomalies** (such as duplicate entries or conflicting interaction logs) that could skew analytics. If the data is inconsistent, the insights generated will be flawed, leading to **incorrect business decisions** and negatively impacting sales strategies. For instance, a team might mistakenly target the wrong customer segment due to corrupted data trends.

steps.title: Maintaining Customer Trust
steps.content: From a holistic perspective, data integrity is essential for maintaining **trust with customers**. Customers provide their personal details with the expectation that the company will handle their information correctly and securely. If a customer notices errors in their own records or experiences service issues due to poor data management, it damages the company's reputation and undermines the professional relationship the CRM is intended to build.

steps.title: Define integrity and decision-making
steps.content: Data integrity refers to the $accuracy$, $consistency$, and $reliability$ of data over its entire lifecycle. In the context of a CRM, this means that every piece of customer information—from phone numbers to purchase history—is correct and remains unchanged unless authorized. When data is integral, the sales team can rely on it to make informed, data-driven business decisions without second-guessing the source.

highlights.text: States that data integrity ensures customer data is accurate, consistent, and reliable, which is crucial for making informed business decisions.

steps.title: Evaluate stakeholder trust risks
steps.content: A major operational risk of poor data integrity is the erosion of customer trust. If a CRM handles data incorrectly (e.g., sending promotional emails to the wrong contact or losing sensitive interaction logs), customers may feel their information is insecure. Maintaining data integrity ensures that customer information is handled securely and accurately, which is essential for preserving the professional reputation of the organization.

highlights.text: Explains that it maintains trust with customers by ensuring their information is handled correctly and securely.

steps.title: Mitigate failed sales strategies
steps.content: From an operational perspective, data integrity is vital to prevent **data anomalies** (such as duplicate records or conflicting interaction histories). If the CRM provides insights based on inconsistent data, the sales team might identify false trends. This leads to the high-risk scenario of the organization investing resources into **failed sales strategies** because the underlying data was flawed. High integrity ensures the validity of these insights, protecting the company from strategic blunders.

highlights.text: Outlines how it prevents data anomalies and inconsistencies that could lead to incorrect insights and negatively impact sales strategies.

parts.content: Explain how encapsulation can be applied to protect customer data within the CRM application.
parts.markscheme:
- **States** that encapsulation involves wrapping customer data within objects and restricting direct access to it, exposing only necessary methods (accessors and mutators). <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explains** that it allows for validation checks when accessing or modifying data, ensuring that only valid data is stored in the system. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Outlines** that by hiding the internal representation of customer data, encapsulation reduces the risk of accidental data corruption and unauthorized access. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define encapsulation and restriction
steps.content:
Encapsulation is a fundamental concept in Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) where we wrap the **customer data** (like names, emails, and phone numbers) and the methods that act on that data into a single unit, known as an object. 

By declaring the data attributes as `private`, we restrict direct access to them from outside the class. This means other parts of the CRM application cannot accidentally or maliciously modify the data without going through the object's interface.

steps.title: Control access via methods
steps.content:
To interact with this restricted data, we provide specific public methods known as **accessors** (getters) and **mutators** (setters). 

Instead of allowing a program to change a customer's balance directly, we use a method like `setBalance()`. This ensures that any interaction with the data is controlled and follows the rules we've established for the CRM system.

steps.title: Implement validation and security
steps.content:
Because data is modified only through mutator methods, we can include **validation checks**. For example, a method to update an email address can verify that the input contains an "@" symbol before saving it. 

This maintains **data integrity** by preventing invalid or corrupt data from entering the system. Furthermore, by hiding the internal details of how the data is stored, we reduce the risk of unauthorized access and accidental data corruption, which are critical for protecting sensitive customer information.

parts.content: Discuss the role of abstraction in simplifying the development and maintenance of the CRM application.
parts.markscheme:
- **States** that abstraction simplifies the development process by focusing on high-level functionalities and hiding complex implementation details. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explains** that it enables the creation of reusable components (e.g., customer data models, interaction modules) for easier maintenance and updates. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Discusses** how abstraction helps manage complexity by breaking the application down into manageable parts, improving understanding and modification. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define abstraction in context
steps.content: In IB Computer Science (**Topic 4.1.8**), abstraction is the process of removing unnecessary details to focus on the essential features of a system. For a CRM application, this means developers can work with high-level concepts like a "Customer" or an "Interaction" without needing to constantly worry about the low-level code that handles database storage or memory management.

steps.title: Focus on high-level functionality
steps.content: Abstraction simplifies the development process because it allows programmers to focus on **high-level functionalities**. Instead of writing complex code for every data entry, they can use abstract methods (like `saveCustomer()`) that **hide complex implementation details**. This allows the team to build the sales insights and tracking tools faster without getting bogged down in the underlying technical mechanics.

highlights.text: states that abstraction simplifies the development process by focusing on high-level functionalities and hiding complex implementation details.

steps.title: Enable reusable components
steps.content: One of the biggest benefits for maintenance is that abstraction enables the creation of **reusable components**. For example, a single 'Customer Data Model' can be used across the sales, support, and marketing modules. If the company needs to update how customer phone numbers are stored, they only need to update that one abstract model rather than searching through the entire application's code.

highlights.text: enables the creation of reusable components (e.g., customer data models, interaction modules) for easier maintenance and updates.

steps.title: Manage system complexity
steps.content: Finally, abstraction helps **manage complexity** by breaking the CRM application down into **manageable parts**. By isolating different functions—such as data storage, user interface, and analytics—into separate abstract layers, the system becomes much easier to understand. This modularity ensures that a modification in the 'Insights' module is unlikely to accidentally break the 'Customer Tracking' module, making long-term maintenance safer and more efficient.

highlights.text: helps manage complexity by breaking the application down into manageable parts, improving understanding and modification.

steps.title: Focusing on High-Level Logic
steps.content:
Abstraction allows the CRM developers to focus on **high-level functionalities**—the "what" of the system—rather than the low-level "how." 

For example, a developer designing a "Sales Insight" feature doesn't need to know the complex implementation details of how data is physically indexed in the database or how memory is managed by the operating system. By hiding these details, the development process is significantly simplified and faster.

highlights.text: focusing on high-level functionalities and hiding complex implementation details

steps.title: Creating Reusable CRM Components
steps.content:
Abstraction enables the creation of **reusable components**, such as a generic **Customer Data Model**. 

This model acts as a template that can be used by both the "Sales Tracking" module and the "Interaction History" module. Because the specific details of data storage are abstracted away, if the company decides to switch from a local database to a cloud-based storage system, they only need to update the internal code of that one model. The rest of the CRM application remains unchanged, making maintenance much easier.

highlights.text: enables the creation of reusable components (e.g., customer data models, interaction modules) for easier maintenance and updates

steps.title: Managing Complexity through Modules
steps.content:
A CRM application is inherently complex, but abstraction helps manage this by breaking the software into **manageable parts** or modules.

By using an **Interaction Module** to handle all customer logs, the development team can modify how calls or emails are tracked without affecting the sales forecasting logic. This modular approach, rooted in **Topic 4.1.8 (Abstraction)**, makes the system easier to understand, test, and modify as the business grows. This directly relates to **Topic 1.1.11**, as well-abstracted systems are easier to update and release.

highlights.text: helps manage complexity by breaking the application down into manageable parts, improving understanding and modification

parts.content: Evaluate the potential challenges of using inheritance in the CRM application when dealing with different types of customer interactions.
parts.markscheme:
- **States** that inheritance can lead to a rigid class hierarchy, making it difficult to accommodate new types of customer interactions without significant refactoring. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explains** that it may result in code duplication or inappropriate inheritance if specific interactions require unique attributes or methods that cannot be easily generalized. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Evaluates** how over-reliance on inheritance can cause tight coupling between classes, reducing the overall flexibility and scalability of the application. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze hierarchy rigidity
steps.content:
Using the **Object-Oriented Design Analysis** approach, we first look at how inheritance enforces a strict "is-a" relationship. In a CRM, if you define a base class `Interaction`, you create a rigid hierarchy. 

If the business later introduces a complex interaction type that doesn't perfectly fit the existing structure (like a multi-channel marketing campaign), it becomes very difficult to accommodate this without **significant refactoring** of the parent class and all its existing children. This lack of fluidity is a major challenge in evolving software.

highlights.text: inheritance can lead to a rigid class hierarchy, making it difficult to accommodate new types of customer interactions without significant refactoring.

steps.title: Evaluate data modeling issues
steps.content:
When analyzing diverse interactions like **emails** and **meetings**, we find they have very different needs. An email needs fields for `subjectLine` and `attachmentSize`, while a meeting needs `roomLocation` and `attendeeList`. 

If we use inheritance to force these into a single tree, we often encounter two problems:
1. **Inappropriate Inheritance**: Subclasses might inherit methods or data they don't actually use (e.g., an Email object inheriting a `getMeetingRoom()` method).
2. **Code Duplication**: If two interaction types share a feature (like "Video Recording") but are in different branches of the inheritance tree, you may end up duplicating the logic because you cannot easily inherit from two parents in many languages.

highlights.text: result in code duplication or inappropriate inheritance if specific interactions require unique attributes or methods that cannot be easily generalized.

steps.title: Assess coupling and scalability
steps.content:
Finally, we must evaluate the impact on system stability. Inheritance creates **tight coupling** between the parent and child classes. 

If a developer changes a method in the base `Interaction` class to fix a bug in Meetings, it might accidentally break the logic for Emails. This dependency reduces the **flexibility and scalability** of the CRM, as every new feature risks breaking existing interaction types due to their shared ancestry.

highlights.text: over-reliance on inheritance can cause tight coupling between classes, reducing the overall flexibility and scalability of the application.

steps.title: Analyze rigid class hierarchies
steps.content: Using inheritance in a CRM application creates a rigid "is-a" relationship between a parent class (like `Interaction`) and its children (like `Email` or `PhoneCall`). From a maintenance perspective, this hierarchy can become problematic if business requirements change. If a new type of interaction is introduced that only partially fits the parent structure, the entire hierarchy may require significant refactoring to accommodate it.

highlights.text: inheritance can lead to a rigid class hierarchy, making it difficult to accommodate new types of customer interactions without significant refactoring

steps.title: Evaluate unique attribute challenges
steps.content: As the CRM scales, different interactions will require unique attributes (e.g., an `Email` needs an attachment field, while a `Meeting` needs a physical location). If these unique needs aren't handled carefully, developers might resort to "inappropriate inheritance," where child classes inherit methods they don't use, or code is duplicated across branches of the tree because the shared parent is too generalized to be useful.

highlights.text: result in code duplication or inappropriate inheritance if specific interactions require unique attributes or methods

steps.title: Assess tight coupling impact
steps.content: The most significant maintenance risk is **tight coupling**. Because child classes are heavily dependent on the implementation details of the parent class, a single change to the base `Interaction` class can cause cascading bugs throughout the entire application. This lack of flexibility makes it difficult to scale the system or swap out components, ultimately reducing the application's long-term sustainability.

highlights.text: over-reliance on inheritance can cause tight coupling between classes, reducing the overall flexibility and scalability

questions.specification: A nationwide retail chain is planning to transition from its traditional local cash register system to a centralized cloud-based inventory and sales management platform.

parts.content: Outline why **technical feasibility** must be considered before the project begins.
parts.markscheme:
- To determine if the organization's existing hardware or network infrastructure can support the requirements of the new cloud-based system <marks>1 mark</marks>
- To identify whether the technical skills required to implement and maintain the system are currently available within the organization <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of **questionnaires** to gather requirements from the large number of employees who will use the new platform.
parts.markscheme:
- Questionnaires allow for the efficient collection of data from a large geographic group of employees simultaneously <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The responses provide quantifiable data that can be easily analyzed to identify common needs or concerns <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, they lack the flexibility of interviews, meaning researchers cannot ask follow-up questions to clarify ambiguous or unexpected answers <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline **one** disadvantage of using **external training** providers to teach staff how to use the new system.
parts.markscheme:
- External training can be very expensive due to high consultancy fees and the cost of travel for staff <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The training materials may be generic and not tailored to the specific business workflows or unique features of the company's internal setup <marks>1 mark</marks>
